analogy: Think of "Understanding Marginalisation" through the chapter's own opening images: When People Revolt 1857 and After 5 Policies and the People In the previous chapters you looked at the policies of the East India Company and the effect they had on different people. Kings, queens, peasants, landlords, tribals, soldiers were all affected in different ways.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Understanding Marginalisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that when People Revolt 1857 and After 5 Policies and the People In the previous chapters you looked at the policies of the East India Company and the effect they had on different people.

The text explains that kings, queens, peasants, landlords, tribals, soldiers were all affected in different ways.

The text explains that you have also seen how people resist policies and actions that harm their interests or go against their sentiments.

The text explains that nawabs lose their power Since the mid-eighteenth century, nawabs and rajas had seen their power erode.

The text explains that they had gradually lost their authority and honour.

The text explains that residents had been stationed in many courts, the freedom of the rulers reduced, their armed forces disbanded, and their revenues and territories taken away by stages.

The text explains that many ruling families tried to negotiate with the Company to protect their interests.

The text explains that for example, Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi wanted the Company to recognise her adopted son as the heir to the kingdom after the death of her husband.

The text explains that 1 – Sepoys and peasants gather forces for the revolt that spread across the plains of north India in 1857

The text explains that 52 OUR PASTS – III Peshwa Baji Rao II, pleaded that he be given his father’s pension when the latter died.

The text explains that however, the Company, confident of its superiority and military powers, turned down these pleas.

The text explains that awadh was one of the last territories to be annexed.

The text explains that in 1801, a subsidiary alliance was imposed on Awadh, and in 1856 it was taken over.

The text explains that governor -General Dalhousie declared that the territory was being misgoverned and British rule was needed to ensure proper administration.

The text explains that the Company even began to plan how to bring the Mughal dynasty to an end.

The text explains that the name of the Mughal king was removed from the coins minted by the Company.

The text explains that when PeOPle RevOlT 53 Source 1 Responses to reforms The British believed that Indian society had to be reformed.

The text explains that through the Eyes of the People To get a glimpse of what people were thinking those days about British rule, study Sources 1 and 2.

The text explains that 3 – Rebel sepoys at Meerut attack officers, enter their homes and set fire to buildings Source 2 contd.

The text explains that through these measures, the British government accepted direct responsibility for ruling India.

The text explains that but after the occupation of Orissa in 1803, the British showed no inclination to oblige him on either score.

The text explains that soon its ripples spread in different directions with Khurda becoming its epicenter.

The text explains that hit by a shell, Henry Lawrence, the Chief Commissioner of Awadh, died in one of the rooms that you see in the picture.

The text explains that notice how buildings carry the marks of past events.

The text explains that what could be the reasons for the confidence of the British rulers about their position in India before May 1857?

The text explains that what impact did Bahadur Shah Zafar’s support to the rebellion have on the people and the ruling families?

The text explains that how did the British succeed in securing the submission of the rebel landowners of Awadh?

The text explains that in what ways did the British change their policies as a result of the rebellion of 1857?

The text explains that find out stories and songs remembered by people in your area or your family about San Sattavan ki Ladaai.

The text explains that what memories do people cherish about the great uprising?

The text explains that find out more about Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi.

The text explains that in what ways would she have been an unusual woman for her times?

Source: NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III, Chapter 5: Understanding Marginalisation

prelims_traps: Do not treat 52 OUR PASTS – III and 54 OUR PASTS – III as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Understanding Marginalisation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Understanding Marginalisation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 52 OUR PASTS – III and 54 OUR PASTS – III to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Understanding Marginalisation, which statement is supported by the source text about 62 OUR PASTS – III?
options: The peasants and the sepoys In the countryside, peasants and zamindars resented the high taxes and the rigid methods of revenue collection.
Many failed to pay back their loans to the moneylenders and gradually lost the lands they had tilled for generations.
The Indian sepoys in the employ of the Company also had reasons for discontent.
They were unhappy about their pay, allowances and conditions of service.
